_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AHA/ASA 2019 acute stroke guideline + 2021 secondary prevention update, ESO 2021 guideline, NEJM DAWN and DEFUSE-3 thrombectomy trials, CDC stroke statistics 2024. Numbers checked against Heart Disease and Stroke Statistics 2024 (Circulation).

h1Title: Cerebral Infarction (Ischemic Stroke): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Cerebral infarction is the death of brain tissue caused by sudden loss of blood supply when an artery to the brain becomes blocked, and it accounts for 87% of all strokes. Roughly 7.6 million ischemic strokes occur globally each year, with the United States recording about 690,000 cases annually, and one in four adults over 25 will have a stroke in their lifetime. The condition presents suddenly with facial droop, arm weakness, slurred speech, vision loss, or loss of balance — symptoms captured in the FAST mnemonic (Face, Arms, Speech, Time). Each minute of large-vessel occlusion destroys roughly 1.9 million neurons, so cerebral infarction is the most time-sensitive emergency in neurology. Intravenous thrombolytics given within 4.5 hours and mechanical thrombectomy performed up to 24 hours in selected patients can reverse disability when delivered fast enough.

keyStats.prevalence: 87% of all strokes are ischemic; ~690,000 ischemic strokes annually in the US (CDC, Heart Disease and Stroke Statistics 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Men have a 25% higher lifetime risk than women, but women have more strokes and worse outcomes due to longer life expectancy; Black adults face roughly double the stroke incidence of white adults
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 71 in men, 76 in women; about 10-15% of strokes now occur in adults under 50
keyStats.globalCases: ~7.6 million ischemic strokes per year worldwide; 101 million stroke survivors living globally (GBD 2019)

definition: Cerebral infarction (ICD-10: I63) is focal brain tissue death caused by sustained interruption of arterial blood flow, depriving neurons of oxygen and glucose for long enough to trigger irreversible necrosis. The infarct core forms within minutes and is surrounded by an ischemic penumbra — hypoperfused but still viable tissue that defines the therapeutic target. Cerebral infarction is one of two subtypes of stroke, the other being intracerebral or subarachnoid hemorrhage; ischemic strokes account for roughly 87% of cases. The condition is classified by mechanism using the TOAST criteria into large-artery atherothrombosis (carotid or intracranial atherosclerosis), cardioembolism (most often atrial fibrillation), small-vessel lacunar infarction, stroke of other determined cause (such as arterial dissection, vasculitis, or hypercoagulable state), and cryptogenic stroke when no source is identified after full workup. Vascular territory determines the clinical syndrome: middle cerebral artery infarcts produce contralateral face-arm weakness and aphasia or neglect, posterior circulation infarcts cause vertigo, diplopia, and ataxia, and anterior cerebral artery infarcts produce leg-dominant weakness. Cerebral infarction is managed acutely by vascular neurologists, interventional neuroradiologists, and stroke-unit teams, with rehabilitation medicine, cardiology, and primary care leading long-term secondary prevention.

typesClassification.description: Caused by atherosclerotic plaque rupture or critical stenosis in the carotid, vertebral, or intracranial arteries. Accounts for roughly 20-25% of ischemic strokes. Often preceded by transient ischemic attacks in the same vascular territory.

A clot formed in the heart travels to the brain and lodges in a cerebral artery. Atrial fibrillation is the dominant source (responsible for roughly 15-20% of all ischemic strokes); other sources include left ventricular thrombus, mechanical valves, and infective endocarditis.

Occlusion of a deep penetrating artery produces a small (<15 mm) subcortical infarct, typically in the basal ganglia, thalamus, internal capsule, or pons. Accounts for roughly 20-25% of ischemic strokes and is driven by chronic hypertension and diabetes.

Includes cervical or intracranial arterial dissection (a leading cause in adults under 50), cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, vasculitis, sickle cell disease, antiphospholipid syndrome, and inherited thrombophilia. Roughly 5% of cases.

No definite cause identified despite full workup. Accounts for 20-30% of ischemic strokes. Many turn out to be embolic strokes of undetermined source (ESUS), with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation discovered on extended cardiac monitoring.

primarySymptoms: Sudden facial droop, typically on one side, with flattening of the nasolabial fold and an asymmetric smile — the 'F' in the FAST mnemonic and present in about 60% of acute strokes.
Sudden arm or leg weakness on one side of the body, ranging from mild drift to complete hemiplegia, often noticed when the patient cannot raise both arms equally.

Sudden speech disturbance — slurred articulation (dysarthria), word-finding difficulty, or inability to understand spoken language (aphasia) — present in roughly 30% of strokes and most common with left middle cerebral artery infarcts.
Sudden vision loss, double vision, or visual field cut in one or both eyes, classically a homonymous hemianopia when the occipital lobe or optic radiation is involved.
Sudden severe vertigo, loss of balance, or inability to walk a straight line, especially when accompanied by other neurologic signs — a hallmark of posterior circulation stroke.
Sudden numbness or loss of sensation on one side of the face, arm, or leg, often described as the limb feeling dead or absent.
Sudden, unusually severe headache, particularly with vomiting or impaired consciousness — although headache is more common in hemorrhagic stroke, it occurs in 15-25% of ischemic strokes.
Confusion, disorientation, or sudden change in level of alertness, especially in older adults where focal deficits may be subtle.
Sudden difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) with coughing or choking on liquids — present in roughly half of acute strokes and a major aspiration-pneumonia risk.
Unilateral neglect — the patient ignores one side of the body or environment, often combing only half their hair or eating only food on one side of the plate, classic for right-hemisphere strokes.
earlyWarningSigns: A transient ischemic attack (TIA) within the prior 90 days — same FAST symptoms but resolving within minutes to 24 hours; carries a 10-20% risk of completed stroke in the following 90 days
A brief episode of monocular vision loss like a curtain coming down (amaurosis fugax), suggesting a carotid embolic source
New-onset atrial fibrillation detected on routine ECG or wearable device monitoring
Carotid bruit on physical exam, especially with risk factors such as smoking and hypertension
Episodes of sudden vertigo, diplopia, or ataxia lasting under an hour — often missed as vestibular complaints but may signal posterior circulation TIA
emergencySigns: Any sudden neurologic deficit — face droop, arm weakness, speech change, vision loss, vertigo, or confusion — lasting more than a few seconds. Call emergency services immediately; do not wait to see if symptoms improve.
Sudden severe headache different from any prior headache, especially with neck stiffness or vomiting — hemorrhagic stroke or subarachnoid hemorrhage must be excluded urgently.
Loss of consciousness, seizure, or sudden coma in a person with stroke risk factors — may indicate brainstem or large hemispheric infarction.
Worsening neurologic deficit during the first 24-72 hours after a known stroke — may indicate stroke progression, malignant edema, or hemorrhagic transformation.
Sudden inability to swallow saliva, with drooling or choking — high risk of aspiration pneumonia, needs nil-by-mouth and urgent swallow assessment.

causes.description: Atherosclerotic plaque in the cervical carotid, vertebral, or intracranial arteries ruptures, exposing thrombogenic material that triggers clot formation. The clot may occlude the artery locally or embolize to a distal branch. Accounts for roughly 20-25% of ischemic strokes.

A clot formed inside the heart — most often the left atrial appendage in atrial fibrillation — dislodges and travels to the cerebral circulation. Cardioembolism causes 15-30% of ischemic strokes and tends to produce larger, more disabling infarcts than other mechanisms.

Chronic hypertension and diabetes damage deep penetrating arterioles, leading to lipohyalinosis and occlusion. The resulting lacunar infarcts are small but strategically located in motor or sensory pathways and account for 20-25% of strokes.

A tear in the intimal layer of the carotid or vertebral artery allows blood to enter the vessel wall, creating a flap that obstructs flow or embolizes distally. Often follows minor neck trauma, chiropractic manipulation, or coughing fits; the leading identifiable cause of stroke in adults under 50.

Clot formation in the dural venous sinuses raises venous pressure and causes infarction with frequent hemorrhagic transformation. Predominantly affects young women on combined oral contraceptives, during pregnancy or postpartum, or with inherited thrombophilia.

Antiphospholipid syndrome, factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A mutation, protein C/S deficiency, malignancy, and nephrotic syndrome all increase clot formation. These should be considered in young patients or those with recurrent strokes despite standard therapy.

A right-to-left intracardiac shunt allows venous clots from the legs or pelvis to bypass the pulmonary filter and reach the brain. Implicated in cryptogenic stroke in younger patients; the RoPE score helps assess attribution.

riskFactors.description: The single largest modifiable risk factor for ischemic stroke. Each 10 mmHg rise in systolic blood pressure increases stroke risk by about 30%. Treatment to under 130/80 mmHg cuts stroke incidence by roughly 35%.

Increases stroke risk five-fold and accounts for 15-20% of all ischemic strokes. Strokes from atrial fibrillation are larger and more disabling. Anticoagulation reduces this risk by approximately 65%.

Doubles stroke risk through accelerated atherosclerosis and small-vessel disease. Tight glycemic control alongside blood pressure and lipid management produces the strongest reductions in stroke recurrence.
Doubles ischemic stroke risk; risk falls 50% within 1 year of cessation and approaches non-smoker levels after 5 years.
Elevated LDL cholesterol drives carotid and intracranial atherosclerosis. High-intensity statin therapy reduces stroke recurrence by roughly 22% in patients with prior ischemic stroke (SPARCL trial).
Stroke incidence doubles with each decade after age 55. Median age at first stroke is 71 in men and 76 in women, though strokes in adults under 50 are rising.

Black adults in the US have roughly double the stroke incidence of white adults at the same age, driven by higher rates of hypertension, diabetes, and sickle cell disease. South Asians show earlier intracranial atherosclerosis.
A first-degree relative with stroke increases personal risk by about 30%. Monogenic causes (CADASIL, Fabry disease, sickle cell) and prothrombotic mutations contribute in younger patients.
BMI ≥30 raises stroke risk by roughly 20%, and sedentary lifestyle independently doubles it. Moderate physical activity 150 minutes weekly lowers stroke incidence by 25-30%.

Consuming more than 4 drinks daily raises ischemic stroke risk by 40-60%. Light-to-moderate intake shows a smaller, debated effect; cardiovascular guidelines no longer endorse alcohol for cardio-protection.

affectedDemographics: US incidence: ~690,000 ischemic strokes annually; stroke is the 5th leading cause of death and the leading cause of long-term adult disability (CDC 2024)
Black adults have age-adjusted stroke incidence roughly 2x that of white adults, with onset 5-10 years earlier on average (REGARDS cohort)
Women account for roughly 60% of stroke deaths, partly because of longer life expectancy; pregnancy and the postpartum period independently raise stroke risk three-fold
About 10-15% of ischemic strokes now occur in adults under 50, often from arterial dissection, paradoxical embolism, or substance use
South Asian populations have higher rates of intracranial atherosclerosis and earlier-age stroke compared with Western European populations
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cerebral infarction is a time-critical, protocol-driven process built around the principle that time is brain — each minute of large-vessel occlusion destroys roughly 1.9 million neurons. On arrival, the stroke team applies the National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS), a 0-42 point neurologic exam that quantifies severity and guides treatment decisions. A non-contrast CT of the head is obtained immediately to rule out intracerebral hemorrhage, which would contraindicate thrombolytics; CT also detects early ischemic changes (the dense MCA sign, loss of grey-white differentiation, sulcal effacement). Most ischemic infarcts are invisible on CT within the first 3-6 hours, and a normal CT in a patient with stroke symptoms does not exclude ischemia. CT angiography of the head and neck is performed concurrently to identify large-vessel occlusion eligible for mechanical thrombectomy. CT perfusion or MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) defines the infarct core and salvageable penumbra and is required for thrombectomy decisions in the 6-24 hour window using DAWN and DEFUSE-3 criteria. Diffusion-weighted MRI is the most sensitive test for early ischemic injury, turning positive within minutes of vessel occlusion. Once acute treatment decisions are made, the workup extends to identify the mechanism: 12-lead ECG and telemetry or extended cardiac monitoring for atrial fibrillation, transthoracic and sometimes transesophageal echocardiography, carotid duplex ultrasound or CT/MR angiography of the cervical vessels, fasting lipid panel, hemoglobin A1c, and — in patients under 50 or with cryptogenic stroke — thrombophilia screen, vasculitis panel, and bubble study for patent foramen ovale.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line emergency imaging — rules out intracerebral hemorrhage (which contraindicates IV thrombolysis) and identifies early ischemic signs. Available 24/7 in most emergency departments and the fastest way to reach a treatment decision.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies on a sliding bed while the CT scanner rotates around the head. The scan takes under 1 minute, no contrast injection required, no metal allowed near the head. Results read by the radiologist in real time.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography (CTA) of the head and neck
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies large-vessel occlusion eligible for mechanical thrombectomy and detects carotid or vertebral stenosis, dissection, and aneurysms. Performed immediately after non-contrast CT when stroke is confirmed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Intravenous iodinated contrast is injected via an arm vein. The scan adds 2-3 minutes to the CT. Mild warmth in the arm during contrast injection is normal. Kidney function is checked first if possible.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive test for ischemic injury, positive within minutes of stroke onset and able to detect infarcts CT misses. Used to define infarct core, assess wake-up strokes, and clarify atypical presentations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies still in a tube-shaped magnet for 20-40 minutes; loud knocking sounds are normal. No metal implants must be confirmed first. No radiation; usually no contrast for stroke protocols.

diagnosticTests.test: CT perfusion or MR perfusion imaging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the ischemic core and salvageable penumbra to guide thrombectomy in the 6-24 hour late window (DAWN and DEFUSE-3 criteria). Software automatically maps tissue at risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Added to a standard CT or MRI with contrast. Takes an extra 3-5 minutes. Output is a color map of the brain showing red (infarcted) and yellow (penumbra) regions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and grades atherosclerotic stenosis of the carotid arteries. Used for secondary-prevention workup; carotid stenosis ≥70% on the symptomatic side may warrant endarterectomy or stenting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel and a probe are applied to the neck for 20-30 minutes while the technician records images and flow velocities. Painless, no radiation, no contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cardiac sources of embolism — left atrial thrombus, valve vegetations, left ventricular thrombus, patent foramen ovale, aortic arch atheroma. TEE is more sensitive for left-atrial appendage and aortic arch sources.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transthoracic: probe on the chest, 30 minutes, painless. Transesophageal: sedation given, probe passed into the esophagus, 30-45 minutes; mild sore throat for a day is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects atrial fibrillation — the dominant cause of cardioembolic stroke. Standard ECG captures only persistent AF; 30-day patch monitors or implantable loop recorders catch paroxysmal AF in 15-30% of cryptogenic strokes (CRYSTAL-AF trial).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 12-lead ECG: stickers on the chest and limbs for 5 minutes. Holter or patch monitor: a small adhesive device worn continuously for days to weeks; daily activities remain normal.

diagnosticTests.test: Fasting lipid panel and hemoglobin A1c
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies dyslipidemia and undiagnosed diabetes, both major modifiable stroke risk factors and central to secondary prevention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, ideally after an 8-12 hour fast for lipids. Results return same day in most centers.

treatmentOverview: Cerebral infarction treatment is organized around three time-critical windows. In the first 4.5 hours from symptom onset, intravenous thrombolysis with alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, maximum 90 mg) is the foundational therapy; tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg, single bolus) is now recommended as an equivalent or preferred alternative by the 2021 AHA/ASA secondary prevention update and the 2024 focused guideline update, based on the AcT, EXTEND-IA TNK, and TIMELESS trials. The earlier thrombolysis is given, the greater the benefit — every 15-minute reduction in treatment delay increases the chance of independent walking at 90 days by roughly 4%. Mechanical thrombectomy with a stent-retriever or aspiration device is the standard of care for large-vessel occlusion of the internal carotid, M1 middle cerebral, or basilar artery. The classic window is up to 6 hours from onset (HERMES meta-analysis pooled five trials showing number needed to treat of 2.6 for one less disabled patient), and selected patients with favorable perfusion imaging can be treated up to 24 hours (DAWN trial showed number needed to treat of 2.8; DEFUSE-3 confirmed benefit out to 16 hours). Thrombolysis and thrombectomy are complementary, not alternatives — eligible patients should receive both, with thrombectomy following intravenous lysis. Outside the reperfusion window, treatment focuses on stroke unit care, dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel for 21 days (CHANCE and POINT trials, for minor stroke and high-risk TIA), high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg), blood pressure control after the first 24-48 hours, anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation typically starting at 4-14 days based on infarct size, glycemic control, swallow assessment before any oral intake, deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis, and early multidisciplinary rehabilitation. Secondary prevention follows the 2021 AHA/ASA guideline: lifestyle modification, antithrombotic therapy matched to mechanism, blood pressure under 130/80 mmHg, LDL under 70 mg/dL, smoking cessation, and treatment of obstructive sleep apnea where present.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg; 10% bolus then 60-minute infusion)
medicalTreatments.description: Recombinant tissue plasminogen activator that dissolves the obstructing clot. Approved within 4.5 hours of symptom onset in eligible patients. Contraindicated with active bleeding, recent surgery, recent stroke, severe uncontrolled hypertension, or hemorrhage on CT.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number needed to treat of approximately 10 for one additional patient with no or minimal disability at 90 days when given within 3 hours; 14 when given between 3 and 4.5 hours (NINDS, ECASS III).

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg, single bolus, max 25 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: A genetically modified tPA variant with higher fibrin specificity and longer half-life, given as a single bolus rather than an hour-long infusion. AHA/ASA 2024 focused update recommends tenecteplase as a reasonable alternative to alteplase within 4.5 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to alteplase for 90-day disability in the AcT (n=1577) and EXTEND-IA TNK trials; faster recanalization before thrombectomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical thrombectomy (stent retriever or aspiration catheter)
medicalTreatments.description: Endovascular removal of clot from large proximal arteries (internal carotid, M1 MCA, basilar). Performed in catheterization labs by neurointerventionalists. Recommended up to 6 hours universally, and 6-24 hours with favorable perfusion imaging per DAWN and DEFUSE-3 criteria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number needed to treat of 2.6 for one less disabled patient at 90 days in the 0-6 hour window (HERMES meta-analysis); 2.8 in the 6-24 hour window (DAWN).

medicalTreatments.name: Dual antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 81 mg + clopidogrel 75 mg daily for 21 days, then aspirin alone
medicalTreatments.description: Used for minor non-cardioembolic stroke (NIHSS ≤3) or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4). Started within 24 hours; clopidogrel loaded with 300-600 mg on day one. Beyond 21 days, monotherapy is preferred to limit bleeding.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 90-day stroke recurrence by roughly 30% versus aspirin alone (CHANCE n=5170, POINT n=4881).

medicalTreatments.name: High-intensity statin: atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Standard of care for secondary prevention after non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke, regardless of baseline LDL. Lowers LDL to under 70 mg/dL; PCSK9 inhibitors added if target is not reached.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrent stroke by 22% over 5 years (SPARCL trial, atorvastatin 80 mg vs placebo).

medicalTreatments.name: Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation in nearly all patients. Preferred over warfarin for lower intracranial hemorrhage risk, no INR monitoring, and fewer drug interactions. Start 4-14 days after acute stroke depending on infarct size.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stroke recurrence by approximately 65% in atrial fibrillation patients (RE-LY, ROCKET-AF, ARISTOTLE, ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48 meta-analysis).

medicalTreatments.name: Blood pressure lowering (ACE inhibitor or ARB plus thiazide; target <130/80 mmHg long-term)
medicalTreatments.description: After the initial 24-48 hours of permissive hypertension, blood pressure is gradually reduced to target. The PROGRESS trial showed perindopril plus indapamide reduces stroke recurrence by 28%.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each 10 mmHg systolic reduction lowers stroke recurrence by approximately 35% (Ettehad meta-analysis, Lancet 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Carotid endarterectomy or stenting for 70-99% symptomatic stenosis
medicalTreatments.description: Surgical or endovascular treatment of severe symptomatic carotid stenosis on the side of the stroke. Best benefit when performed within 2 weeks of the index event. Endarterectomy is preferred in older patients; stenting in younger patients with surgically difficult anatomy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Endarterectomy reduces 5-year stroke risk by 16% absolute in symptomatic 70-99% stenosis (NASCET trial); number needed to treat of 6.

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive hemicraniectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of a large bone flap to relieve life-threatening cerebral edema after malignant middle cerebral artery infarction. Performed within 48 hours of symptom onset in patients under 60 (and selected older patients) per pooled DECIMAL, DESTINY, and HAMLET data.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces mortality from approximately 78% to 29% and roughly doubles the chance of moderate disability survival; functional outcomes remain limited.

surgicalOptions.name: Carotid endarterectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque from the carotid bifurcation. Indicated for 70-99% symptomatic stenosis ipsilateral to the stroke, ideally within 2 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Perioperative stroke/death risk approximately 5-6%; 16% absolute 5-year stroke reduction in symptomatic high-grade stenosis (NASCET).

surgicalOptions.name: Carotid artery stenting
surgicalOptions.description: Endovascular alternative to endarterectomy in patients with surgically inaccessible anatomy, previous neck radiation, or high surgical risk. Performed with embolic protection devices.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term stroke prevention comparable to endarterectomy in CREST and ICSS trials; slightly higher periprocedural stroke risk but lower myocardial infarction risk.

surgicalOptions.name: Left atrial appendage closure (Watchman or Amulet device)
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous occlusion of the left atrial appendage in atrial fibrillation patients who cannot tolerate long-term anticoagulation. Performed under general anesthesia with transesophageal guidance.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Non-inferior to warfarin for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation in PROTECT AF and PREVAIL trials; reduces long-term bleeding risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cardiac rehabilitation-style supervised exercise programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured aerobic and resistance exercise after stroke improves cardiovascular fitness, lowers blood pressure and LDL, and reduces recurrence risk. Recommended in AHA/ASA secondary prevention guidelines.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean or DASH dietary pattern
alternativeTreatments.description: The PREDIMED trial showed a Mediterranean diet supplemented with olive oil or nuts reduces stroke by roughly 33% over 5 years compared with a low-fat control diet. DASH similarly lowers blood pressure and stroke risk.

whySeeSpecialist: A vascular neurologist or stroke neurologist should be involved in every acute ischemic stroke for treatment decision-making (thrombolysis, thrombectomy, antithrombotic selection) and for guideline-directed secondary prevention. Interventional neuroradiologists or neurointerventional surgeons perform thrombectomy. Cardiology is essential when atrial fibrillation, PFO closure, or left atrial appendage occlusion are involved. Long-term care typically returns to primary care once secondary prevention is stable, with periodic neurology follow-up.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek certified stroke centers (Joint Commission Primary Stroke Center, Thrombectomy-Capable Stroke Center, or Comprehensive Stroke Center). Verify the center provides 24/7 thrombolysis, has neurointerventional coverage for thrombectomy, and admits to a dedicated stroke unit — stroke unit care alone reduces death or disability by roughly 20%. For outpatient follow-up, choose a vascular neurology clinic that monitors blood pressure, LDL, A1c, and adherence at every visit, and that runs structured rehabilitation referrals.
preventionStrategies: Control blood pressure to under 130/80 mmHg — the single largest stroke-prevention intervention; each 10 mmHg systolic reduction cuts stroke risk by roughly 35%
Treat atrial fibrillation with direct oral anticoagulants when CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women — reduces stroke risk by approximately 65%
Stop smoking completely — stroke risk falls 50% within the first year and approaches non-smoker levels after 5 years
Maintain LDL cholesterol under 100 mg/dL (under 70 mg/dL after a stroke or TIA) using high-intensity statin therapy
Manage diabetes with hemoglobin A1c under 7% where safe, alongside blood pressure and lipid optimization
Maintain physical activity of at least 150 minutes weekly of moderate aerobic exercise; this lowers stroke incidence by 25-30%
lifestyleModifications: Adopt a Mediterranean or DASH dietary pattern — both have randomized-trial evidence for reducing stroke and cardiovascular events
Limit alcohol to under 1 drink per day for women and 2 for men; avoid binge drinking entirely as it acutely raises stroke risk
Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week, plus 2 sessions of resistance training
Sustain weight loss of 5-10% if overweight — improves blood pressure, lipids, and glycemic control simultaneously
Manage obstructive sleep apnea with CPAP if diagnosed; untreated apnea independently raises stroke risk roughly 2-fold
Take all secondary prevention medications consistently — adherence is the strongest predictor of avoiding a second stroke
Track blood pressure at home; share readings with the clinician to refine antihypertensive therapy

recommended: Olive oil as the primary fat source; nuts (30 g daily) as an evening or breakfast addition
Fresh vegetables and fruits (5+ servings daily) emphasizing leafy greens and berries linked to vascular health
Whole grains (oats, brown rice, whole wheat) replacing refined carbohydrates
Fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) 2-3 servings per week for omega-3 intake
Legumes and pulses several times weekly as plant protein
Low-sodium diet (under 2,300 mg/day, ideally 1,500 mg for blood pressure control)
avoid: Processed and red meats consumed daily — increase stroke risk through saturated fat and sodium
Sugar-sweetened beverages and pastries that drive obesity and worsen glycemic control
Trans fats from partially hydrogenated oils — banned in many countries but still present in some packaged foods
Excess sodium from canned soups, processed snacks, and restaurant meals
Binge alcohol consumption — more than 4 drinks in one sitting acutely raises stroke risk
Energy drinks and high-dose stimulants in patients with arrhythmia or uncontrolled hypertension

exerciseGuidelines: After medical clearance and during stroke rehabilitation, supervised aerobic exercise such as treadmill walking, cycling, or aquatic therapy is recommended starting at low intensity and progressing as tolerance allows. Resistance training of unaffected and paretic limbs improves strength and balance. Once secondary prevention is stable, target 150 minutes weekly of moderate-intensity aerobic activity, divided across most days, plus 2 resistance-training sessions. Activities that strongly elevate blood pressure (heavy isometric lifts, breath-holding maneuvers) should be reviewed with the clinician.
dailyManagement: Take antithrombotic, antihypertensive, and statin medications at the same time each day — missed doses are the leading reversible cause of recurrent stroke
Check blood pressure at home several times per week and log readings; flag systolic above 140 or below 100 mmHg to the clinician
Monitor for warning signs of recurrence using FAST and call emergency services immediately if any new deficit appears
Attend scheduled rehabilitation sessions; home practice between sessions drives most of the functional gains
Use a pillbox or medication app to confirm adherence — especially important after cognitive or language deficits
Maintain hydration and a balanced diet; manage swallowing precautions if dysphagia persists
prognosis: Functional outcome after cerebral infarction depends on the size and location of the infarct, the speed and success of reperfusion therapy, and the quality of stroke unit and rehabilitation care. The 90-day modified Rankin Scale (mRS) is the standard outcome measure: an mRS of 0-2 (independent ambulation, able to manage personal affairs) is considered a good outcome. After successful thrombectomy in large-vessel occlusion, roughly 46% of patients reach mRS 0-2 versus 27% with medical therapy alone (HERMES). Mortality at 30 days is approximately 10% for ischemic stroke overall, rising to 20-30% for large hemispheric or basilar artery infarcts. About 25% of stroke survivors have a recurrent stroke within 5 years without optimal secondary prevention; guideline-directed prevention cuts this in half. Most neurologic recovery occurs in the first 3-6 months, though continued gains, particularly in language and gait, occur up to 12-18 months. The strongest individual predictors of good long-term outcome are younger age, low baseline NIHSS, successful reperfusion (mTICI 2b-3), and adherence to secondary prevention.
recoveryTimeline: First 24-72 hours: stroke unit care, neurologic monitoring, decisions about decompressive surgery if malignant edema develops. Days 4-14: initiation of secondary prevention (antithrombotic, statin, blood pressure), early mobilization, swallow assessment, and rehabilitation planning. Weeks 2-12: most rapid motor and language recovery, typically in an inpatient rehabilitation facility or intensive outpatient program. Months 3-6: continued functional gains and return to work for milder strokes. Months 6-18: slower but ongoing recovery, especially of gait, fine motor skills, and language; aphasia and hemiparesis can keep improving with targeted therapy. Lifelong: secondary prevention medications, blood pressure and lipid monitoring, and surveillance for recurrence.
complications: Hemorrhagic transformation of the infarct, occurring in 6-10% of patients overall and roughly twice that after thrombolysis; symptomatic hemorrhage carries 50% mortality
Malignant cerebral edema after large MCA or cerebellar infarction — peaks at days 2-5, presents with declining consciousness and may require decompressive hemicraniectomy
Aspiration pneumonia from dysphagia — the leading cause of medical mortality after stroke; prevented by nil-by-mouth until formal swallow evaluation
Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism due to immobility — reduced by intermittent pneumatic compression and prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin once hemorrhage is excluded
Post-stroke depression, affecting roughly 30% of survivors and a major barrier to rehabilitation engagement; SSRIs are first-line treatment
Recurrent stroke — approximately 25% within 5 years without optimal secondary prevention; cut in half by guideline-directed therapy
Vascular cognitive impairment and post-stroke dementia, affecting 20-30% of survivors at 1 year; risk rises with multiple infarcts and white-matter disease
Spasticity, contractures, and shoulder subluxation in paretic limbs — prevented by early positioning, range-of-motion exercises, and botulinum toxin where needed

supportResources.name: American Stroke Association — Stroke Resources
supportResources.description: Patient education, recovery resources, support groups, and FAST campaign materials from the AHA division dedicated to stroke.

supportResources.name: World Stroke Organization
supportResources.description: International advocacy, global stroke statistics, and patient-facing education in multiple languages.

supportResources.name: National Stroke Association (now part of American Stroke Association)
supportResources.description: Survivor support, rehabilitation resources, and stroke-survivor community.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seizure (Todd's Paralysis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seizures can cause focal weakness mimicking stroke, especially the post-ictal Todd's paralysis after a partial seizure. Stroke onset is abrupt with maximal deficit at the start; seizure deficits are often preceded by automatisms or jerking, and weakness gradually improves over minutes to hours. EEG and MRI help distinguish them when the diagnosis is unclear.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bell's Palsy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bell's palsy is a peripheral facial nerve palsy that weakens the entire half of the face including the forehead. Stroke causes central facial weakness that spares the forehead because of bilateral cortical innervation to the upper face. Stroke is also accompanied by arm or leg weakness or speech changes; isolated facial weakness with forehead involvement is almost never a stroke.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine with Aura
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migrainous aura produces gradual (5-20 minute) spreading neurologic symptoms — visual scintillations, then numbness, then language change — followed by headache. Stroke is sudden and maximal at onset, without the slow march of symptoms. When in doubt, treat as stroke and image.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypoglycemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe hypoglycemia can cause focal neurologic signs that mimic stroke. A bedside glucose check is mandatory in every suspected stroke, and symptoms resolve with glucose correction. Stroke deficits do not improve with glucose administration.

relatedConditions.name: Atrial Fibrillation
relatedConditions.relevance: The dominant cause of cardioembolic stroke, responsible for 15-20% of all ischemic strokes. Anticoagulation in eligible patients reduces stroke risk by approximately 65%.

relatedConditions.name: Hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: The single largest modifiable risk factor for ischemic stroke; every 10 mmHg rise in systolic pressure increases stroke risk by roughly 30%.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Doubles ischemic stroke risk through accelerated atherosclerosis and small-vessel disease; integrated control of glycemia, lipids, and blood pressure is essential.

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Reduced left ventricular function predisposes to mural thrombus and atrial fibrillation, both significant cardioembolic stroke sources.

relatedConditions.name: Post-Stroke Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Structured multidisciplinary rehabilitation is the cornerstone of functional recovery; most gains occur in the first 3-6 months after the stroke.

faqs.question: What is the FAST test for stroke?
faqs.answer: FAST stands for Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, and Time to call emergency services. Ask the person to smile, raise both arms, and repeat a sentence. If any are abnormal, call emergency services and note the time symptoms started.

faqs.question: How is ischemic stroke different from hemorrhagic stroke?
faqs.answer: Ischemic stroke is caused by a blocked artery cutting off blood supply to part of the brain; hemorrhagic stroke is caused by a ruptured vessel bleeding into the brain. About 87% of strokes are ischemic. A non-contrast CT distinguishes them; treatment differs sharply.

faqs.question: How long do I have to get treatment for a stroke?
faqs.answer: Intravenous thrombolytics work up to 4.5 hours from symptom onset, and mechanical thrombectomy up to 6 hours, or 24 hours in selected patients with favorable imaging. Every 15-minute delay reduces the chance of a good recovery.

faqs.question: What does 'time is brain' mean?
faqs.answer: During a large-vessel stroke, roughly 1.9 million neurons die every minute the artery remains blocked. The longer the delay to reperfusion, the worse the long-term disability. Minutes saved translate directly into preserved brain function.

faqs.question: What is a TIA and how is it different from a stroke?
faqs.answer: A transient ischemic attack (TIA) produces stroke symptoms that resolve within 24 hours without permanent brain injury. TIA is a warning: 10-20% of patients have a completed stroke within 90 days. Seek emergency evaluation even if symptoms resolve.

faqs.question: Can young adults have a stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 10-15% of ischemic strokes occur in adults under 50. Common causes include arterial dissection, paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale, hypercoagulable disorders, substance use, and migraine-related events. Workup is more extensive in younger patients.

faqs.question: What is mechanical thrombectomy?
faqs.answer: Mechanical thrombectomy removes a large clot from a brain artery using a stent retriever or aspiration catheter threaded from the groin or wrist under X-ray guidance. It is standard care for large-vessel occlusion within 6 hours, and up to 24 hours in selected cases.

faqs.question: How effective is tPA for stroke?
faqs.answer: Alteplase within 4.5 hours roughly doubles the chance of minimal or no disability at 90 days. Number needed to treat is about 10 in the 0-3 hour window and 14 in the 3-4.5 hour window. Tenecteplase, a single-bolus alternative, is at least as effective.

faqs.question: What is the NIHSS scale?
faqs.answer: The NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is a 0-42 point neurologic exam quantifying stroke severity across consciousness, gaze, vision, facial movement, motor strength, sensation, language, and inattention. Above 6 indicates moderate-to-severe stroke; above 25 indicates very severe deficits.

faqs.question: What is a good outcome after stroke?
faqs.answer: Outcome is measured at 90 days using the modified Rankin Scale (mRS). An mRS of 0-2 (good outcome) means independent walking and managing daily affairs. Roughly 46% of large-vessel stroke patients reach mRS 0-2 after successful thrombectomy, versus 27% on medical therapy.

faqs.question: Will I have another stroke?
faqs.answer: Without optimal secondary prevention, roughly 25% of stroke survivors have a recurrent stroke within 5 years. Guideline-directed therapy — antithrombotics, statins, blood pressure control, smoking cessation, AF treatment — cuts that risk roughly in half.

faqs.question: How long does stroke recovery take?
faqs.answer: Most neurologic recovery happens in the first 3-6 months, with the fastest gains in weeks 2-12. Continued improvement in language, gait, and fine motor skills occurs up to 12-18 months with targeted rehabilitation. Consistent therapy drives most long-term gains.

faqs.question: Can stress cause a stroke?
faqs.answer: Acute emotional stress can transiently raise blood pressure and trigger a stroke in vulnerable individuals, though absolute per-episode risk is low. Chronic stress contributes to hypertension and unhealthy behaviors that raise stroke risk over years.

faqs.question: What blood pressure level prevents another stroke?
faqs.answer: Guidelines target under 130/80 mmHg long-term in stroke survivors. PROGRESS showed perindopril plus indapamide reduces stroke recurrence by 28%, and each 10 mmHg systolic reduction cuts recurrence by about 35%. Combination therapy is often needed.

faqs.question: Should everyone with a stroke take a blood thinner?
faqs.answer: Antithrombotic choice depends on mechanism. Non-cardioembolic strokes get antiplatelets (aspirin, clopidogrel, or dual therapy for 21 days in minor stroke). Cardioembolic strokes from atrial fibrillation get anticoagulants. Mixing both is avoided due to bleeding risk.

faqs.question: What is a wake-up stroke?
faqs.answer: A wake-up stroke is discovered on awakening, with unknown onset time. MRI diffusion-FLAIR mismatch or CT perfusion identifies wake-up strokes with salvageable tissue. WAKE-UP and EXTEND trials showed these patients benefit from reperfusion therapy when imaging is favorable.

faqs.question: Does stroke cause depression?
faqs.answer: Post-stroke depression affects roughly 30% of survivors as a biological consequence of brain injury and a response to disability. Untreated, it worsens functional outcome. SSRIs are first-line therapy and benefit both mood and motor recovery. Screen at every follow-up visit.

faqs.question: Can I fly after a stroke?
faqs.answer: Most patients can fly safely 2-4 weeks after an uncomplicated ischemic stroke if stable. Avoid travel during the first 2 weeks when complications and recurrence risk peak. Long-haul flights raise venous thrombosis risk and warrant compression stockings and mobilization.

faqs.question: How is stroke treated when atrial fibrillation is the cause?
faqs.answer: Acutely, thrombolysis or thrombectomy if within window. Long-term, an oral anticoagulant such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, or dabigatran, typically started 4-14 days after stroke depending on infarct size. Left atrial appendage closure is an option when anticoagulation is contraindicated.

faqs.question: Are stroke and heart attack the same thing?
faqs.answer: No. Both involve a blocked artery causing tissue death, but stroke affects the brain and heart attack affects heart muscle. They share risk factors (hypertension, smoking, diabetes, cholesterol) and many preventive treatments. Risk-factor control reduces both.

faqs.question: How much does stroke care cost?
faqs.answer: In the US, acute ischemic stroke admission averages USD 20,000 for medical management and over USD 50,000 with thrombectomy; lifetime costs average around USD 140,000 per survivor (AHA 2024). Emerging-market costs are lower but a larger share of income, making prevention valuable.

metaDescription: Cerebral infarction: ischemic stroke from blocked brain arteries. FAST signs, tPA, thrombectomy, recovery, and prevention reviewed by our medical board.

images.altText: Diagram showing the middle cerebral artery territory infarct in the brain, with the affected region shaded
images.caption: Middle cerebral artery territory infarction — the most common large-vessel ischemic stroke pattern.

images.altText: Non-contrast CT of the head showing a hypodense middle cerebral artery territory infarct with loss of grey-white differentiation
images.caption: Non-contrast CT showing an established right middle cerebral artery infarct — hypodensity and loss of grey-white differentiation.

images.altText: Diffusion-weighted MRI of the brain showing a bright acute ischemic stroke lesion in the right middle cerebral artery territory
images.caption: Diffusion-weighted MRI — the most sensitive test for early cerebral infarction, showing acute ischemic injury within minutes.

images.altText: Anatomical diagram of the Circle of Willis showing the cerebral arteries that supply blood to the brain
images.caption: The Circle of Willis — cerebral artery anatomy that determines the territory and clinical syndrome of an ischemic stroke.
